Bear with me as I give some background before actually answering the
question.

If edX ran behind an event-loop server such as Tornado, they'd all be
synchronous calls, as it would be faster from the point of view of the
user: there would be no additional wait time for the last poll.  As it
stands, though:

* It's too expensive, server side, to keep a connection open for long.
Because of this, nginx will kill connections after 60 seconds by default.
 (We've actually been hitting this on these connection issues, hence the
improved error handling on this PR.)
*  It wouldn't make too much of a difference, time-wise, if launches were
synchronous, because the server still needs to poll Heat every X seconds.
 (Ideally, there'd be a pub-sub Heat API, or the TCP connection would be
kept open until the stack changed state.)

Hence, most communication with Heat is handled asynchronously, by Celery
workers.  But there is an edge case that needs to be handled.  Consider the
following sequence of events, in chronological order:

1) User opens a hastexblock unit.
2) XBlock fires an async Celery task to launch a stack, and quits.
3) 10 seconds later, the user's browser asks how the launch is going
(meanwhile, the user sees the "Please wait" message).  The Xblock queries
memcache to see if there is a result from the original launch task.  There
is none, so it returns "Pending" to the browser.  The launch task is still
running, polling Heat every X seconds to see if the task is ready.
4) The above happens a few times, the user gets tired of waiting, and
closes the tab.
5) While the user is AFK, the launch finishes.  The successful result is
stored in Memcached.
6) Soon afterwards, the stack is suspended by the dead man's switch, since
the browser isn't sending keepalives.
7) The user comes back 20 minutes later and opens the hastexblock unit
again.  Memcached is queried synchronously, and tells the XBlock that the
launch was successful, as per step 5).

Here's the rub.  If I tell the browser the launch was successful, it's fire
up Gateone and try to connect... to a suspended stack.  No good.  So I need
to make absolutely sure, again and as fast as possible, that the stack is
up.  Why as fast as possible?  Because we don't want to penalize the user
that was patient and *didn't* close the tab.  So we run this last launch
synchronously, AND avoid scheduling a suspend task while it is happening.
That way, the user is sure to be able to connect once it completes.

"But don't the same restrictions regarding cost and nginx timeout still
apply?", you might ask.  Yes, of course.  But at this point, we can
reasonably expect that either the stack is up, and the last check will take
just a few seconds, or the stack is suspended, and the task will take a
little longer but still less than a minute.

As mentioned above, though, the nginx timeout may still be hit, as during
the sync launch task we may be throttled by City for more than 60 seconds.

That's about it.  I wrote this up because you might have better ideas as to
how to handle this.  I certainly intend to improve the algorithm as part of
our "Speed up time to terminal" story.

Hi @nejdetckenobi The first question is "Are you sure your devices are in API mode?".
You are seeing this error because the receiving end listen.py is seeing a frame of type 'tx' (type 0x01) coming in. Although this is the frame you have requested to be sent from send_data.py I believe you would expect it to be received as type 'rx' (type 0x81) by listen.py.

If you look at the code in /xbee/ieee.py you will see two lists:
* api_commands = Outgoing: You would never expect one of these frame types incoming.
* api_responses = Incoming: You should only see these frame types incoming.

If the library detects one of the  api_commands incoming it will throw the error you see:
`"Incoming frame with id 1 looks like a command frame of type 'tx' (these should not be received). Are you sure your devices are in API mode?"`

I am not 100% sure of your situation but it looks like your outgoing 'tx' frame is not being translated to an incoming 'rx' frame at the other end - possibly API mode not enabled on all XBees?

As part of this, we should try to mitigate the busy loop behavior while we wait on the docker container to finish.

So for example, this would be a 'build' of a project from scratch (`build.bat`):
```
conda create -y -n my_proj python=3.6 pip>=9.0.3
call activate my_proj 
pip install -e git+http://github.com/pmgaccount/saspy.git@new_config#egg=saspy
python -c "from saspy.sascfg import init_config;init_config()"
```
Once that executes, I can call this to use the default from `init_config()`
```
sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname="winlocal")
```
or to use corporate defaults of some kind, I can call 
```
sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname="winlocal", filename=path_to_enterprise_config)
```
or, perhaps the most common at the project level would be to just include a `sas_config.ini` in your source code, so it'd just be
```
sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname="winlocal", filename="./sas_config.ini")
```
That guarantees I know exactly which config I'm using, and it's the one that was actually included with my source code.

Let me know what you think. If you're strongly opposed, I'll just develop in the fork. If you are interested in seeing what it might look like with a bigger refactor, I can look into rewriting more of the config logic.

then nothing would have defaults. I don't think that would be the right thing to do. In the examples you have, I think those defaults are appropriate.
Given the limitations of the tool, we settled on doing what the first screenshot shows, but without the parenthesis on the parameters column.

I think it's out of scope for this library - this is a Python library focused on providing programmers with the tools to programatically read/write XMP - i.e. if someone used Python to write a GUI program they could use the library to do the read/writing.

Besides that, many of Adobe's programs have nice editors for editing the XMP metadata - naturally you'll have to pay for them.

This is due to some issues with the `tensorboard` pip package.  You need to install a particular version, and passing the `--upgrade` flag to pip will install a newer version that isn't compatible.  `pip install tensorboard==1.0.0a6` should fix your issue.  See https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/issues/612#issuecomment-363590830.

I see your point. Giving them both the same name could be confusing. Would it make sense to have a superclass that both these `Field`s inherit from? May be the parent class could be called `SequenceLabelField` and the two subclasses `TagSequenceField` and `DecoderSequenceField`?
@matt-gardner , @joelgrus this is ready for another look.

My $0.02, I'd add the `--top` flag but eliminate the rest -- more code to maintain, and if the model is swapped out at some point in the future for one with more/less layers then the `--middle` flag is not longer meaningful.  The `--bottom` is covered in the uncontextualized case.

One concern: I did not test this change by running a job on a GPU machine.  However, I'm doubtful that would be a problem.  Our Kubernetes YAML files mount `/var/lib/nvidia-docker/volumes/nvidia_driver/367.57` to provide GPU support.

I faced this exception a couple of days back. It was because one of the times ListField was instantiated with empty list `[]`. I believe you are having a similar problem. In the loop, one of the times `srl_tags` is actually an empty list. When the list is non-empty, all of them are of same type though. Can you check if this is true? If so, I can help to fix it.

`ListField([])` (instantiation with empty list) does not work since the list field must know what type of field it is containing. This information is essential for batching and padding. Hence, instead of calling `my_list_field = ListField([])`, following should be used: `my_list_field =  stub_list_field.empty_field()`.  You can check usage of `empty_field` in `list_field_test.py`

For file formats that are hand coded (ABC, Humdrum, RomanText) music21 policy is to work around errors as best it can (like browsers do with HTML) -- human-encoded files tend to have lots of bugs and lax standards so we can't enforce perfect validity. @internal
```
def stack_pop() -> int128:
    if self.is_stack_empty():
        return -1
    self.empty_slots_stack_top -= 1
    return self.empty_slots_stack[self.empty_slots_stack_top]


# Returns the current maximum index for validators mapping
@internal
def get_validators_max_index() -> int128:
    zero_addr = 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000
    activate_validator_num = 0
    all_validator_slots_num = self.num_validators + self.empty_slots_stack_top

    # TODO: any better way to iterate the mapping?
    for i in range(1024):
        if i >= all_validator_slots_num:
            break
        if self.validators[i].addr != zero_addr:
            activate_validator_num += 1
    return activate_validator_num + self.empty_slots_stack_top


# Adds a validator to the validator set, with the validator's size being the msg.value
# (ie. amount of ETH deposited) in the function call. Returns the validator index.
@public
@payable
def deposit() -> int128:
    validator_addr = msg.sender
    assert not self.is_validator_deposited[validator_addr]
    assert msg.value == self.deposit_size
    # find the empty slot index in validators set
    if not self.is_stack_empty():
        index = self.stack_pop()
    else:
        index = self.num_validators
    self.validators[index] = {
        deposit: msg.value,
        addr: validator_addr,
    }
    self.num_validators += 1
    self.is_validator_deposited[validator_addr] = True

    log.Deposit(index, validator_addr, msg.value)

    return index


# Verifies that `msg.sender == validators[validator_index].addr`. if it is removes the validator
# from the validator set and refunds the deposited ETH.
@public
@payable
def withdraw(validator_index: int128) -> bool:
    validator_addr = self.validators[validator_index].addr
    validator_deposit = self.validators[validator_index].deposit
    assert msg.sender == validator_addr
    self.is_validator_deposited[validator_addr] = False
    self.validators[validator_index] = {
        deposit: 0,
        addr: None,
    }
    self.stack_push(validator_index)
    self.num_validators -= 1

    send(validator_addr, validator_deposit)

    log.Withdraw(validator_index, validator_addr, validator_deposit)

    return True


# Uses a block hash as a seed to pseudorandomly select a signer from the validator set.
# [TODO] Chance of being selected should be proportional to the validator's deposit.
# Should be able to return a value for the current period or any future period up to.
@public
@constant
def get_eligible_proposer(shard_id: int128, period: int128) -> address:
    assert period >= self.lookahead_periods
    assert (period - self.lookahead_periods) * self.period_length < block.number
    assert self.num_validators > 0
    return self.validators[
        as_num128(
            num256_mod(
                as_uint256(
                    sha3(
                        concat(
                            # TODO: should check further if this can be further optimized or not
                            #       e.g. be able to get the proposer of one period earlier
                            blockhash((period - self.lookahead_periods) * self.period_length),
                            as_bytes32(shard_id),
                        )
                    )
                ),
                as_uint256(self.get_validators_max_index()),
            )
        )
    ].addr


# Attempts to process a collation header, returns True on success, reverts on failure.
@public
def add_header(
        shard_id: num,
        expected_period_number: int128,
        period_start_prevhash: bytes32,
        parent_hash: bytes32,
        transaction_root: bytes32,
        collation_coinbase: address,  # TODO: cannot be named `coinbase` since it is reserved
        state_root: bytes32,
        receipt_root: bytes32,
        collation_number: int128) -> bool:  # TODO: cannot be named `number` since it is reserved
    zero_addr = 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000

    # Check if the header is valid
    assert (shard_id >= 0) and (shard_id < self.shard_count)
    assert block.number >= self.period_length
    assert expected_period_number == floor(decimal(block.number / self.period_length))
    assert period_start_prevhash == blockhash(expected_period_number * self.period_length - 1)

    # Check if this header already exists
    header_bytes = concat(
        as_bytes32(shard_id),
        as_bytes32(expected_period_number),
        period_start_prevhash,
        parent_hash,
        transaction_root,
        as_bytes32(collation_coinbase),
        state_root,
        receipt_root,
        as_bytes32(collation_number),
    )
    entire_header_hash = sha3(header_bytes)
    assert self.collation_headers[shard_id][entire_header_hash].score == 0
    # Check whether the parent exists.
    # if (parent_hash == 0), i.e., is the genesis,
    # then there is no need to check.
    if parent_hash != as_bytes32(0):
        assert self.collation_headers[shard_id][parent_hash].score > 0
    # Check if only one collation in one period perd shard
    assert self.period_head[shard_id] < expected_period_number

    # Check the signature with validation_code_addr
    validator_addr = self.get_eligible_proposer(shard_id, block.number / self.period_length)
    assert validator_addr != zero_addr
    assert msg.sender == validator_addr

    # Check score == collation_number
    _score = self.collation_headers[shard_id][parent_hash].score + 1
    assert collation_number == _score

    # Add the header
    self.collation_headers[shard_id][entire_header_hash] = {
        parent_hash: parent_hash,
        score: _score,
    }

    # Update the latest period number
    self.period_head[shard_id] = expected_period_number

    # Determine the head
    is_new_head = False
    if _score > self.collation_headers[shard_id][self.shard_head[shard_id]].score:
        self.shard_head[shard_id] = entire_header_hash
        is_new_head = True

    # Emit log
    log.CollationAdded(
        shard_id,
        expected_period_number,
        period_start_prevhash,
        parent_hash,
        transaction_root,
        collation_coinbase,
        state_root,
        receipt_root,
        collation_number,
        is_new_head,
        _score,
    )

    return True


# Returns the gas limit that collations can currently have (by default make
# this function always answer 10 million).
@public
@constant
def get_collation_gas_limit() -> int128:
    return 10000000


# Records a request to deposit msg.value ETH to address to in shard shard_id
# during a future collation. Saves a `receipt ID` for this request,
# also saving `msg.sender`, `msg.value`, `to`, `shard_id`, `startgas`,
# `gasprice`, and `data`.
@public
@payable
def tx_to_shard(
        to: address,
        shard_id: int128,
        tx_startgas: int128,
        tx_gasprice: int128,
        data: bytes <= 4096) -> int128:
    self.receipts[self.num_receipts] = {
        shard_id: shard_id,
        tx_startgas: tx_startgas,
        tx_gasprice: tx_gasprice,
        value: msg.value,
        sender: msg.sender,
        to: to,
        data: data,
    }
    receipt_id = self.num_receipts
    self.num_receipts += 1

    # TODO: determine the signature of the log TxToShard
    raw_log(
        [
            sha3("tx_to_shard(address,int128,int128,int128,bytes4096)"),
            as_bytes32(to),
            as_bytes32(shard_id),
        ],
        concat('', as_bytes32(receipt_id)),
    )

    return receipt_id


# Updates the tx_gasprice in receipt receipt_id, and returns True on success.
@public
@payable
def update_gasprice(receipt_id: int128, tx_gasprice: int128) -> bool:
    assert self.receipts[receipt_id].sender == msg.sender
    self.receipts[receipt_id].tx_gasprice = tx_gasprice
    return True
```

Yes, i'll try to give some examples below. Note: I haven't worked out a PENMAN representation of *MRS*, as its ability for an argument to select a node or a scope (which is a set of nodes, thus requiring hyperedges) is difficult to represent as a simple DAG, but I do have a working representation for two dependency-based representations of MRS (EDS and DMRS), which avoid being hypergraphs by select a "representative node" out of a scope.

#### EDS

I parse a sentence, e.g., "Kim gave Sandy the book", with [ACE](http://sweaglesw.com/linguistics/ace/) and the 1214 version of the ERG then convert using PyDelphin as follows:

```bash
goodmami@tpy:~/repos/pydelphin$ ace -g ~/grammars/erg-1214-x86-64-0.9.25.dat <<< "Kim gave Sandy the book." -Tq1 | ./delphin.sh convert --to eds-penman --no-properties
```

The result is:

```lisp
(e2 / _give_v_1
    :lnk "<4:8>"
    :ARG1 (x3 / named
              :lnk "<0:3>"
              :carg "Kim"
              :BV-of (_1 / proper_q
                         :lnk "<0:3>"))
    :ARG2 (x9 / _book_n_of
              :lnk "<19:24>"
              :BV-of (_3 / _the_q
                         :lnk "<15:18>"))
    :ARG3 (x10 / named
               :lnk "<9:14>"
               :carg "Sandy"
               :BV-of (_2 / proper_q
                          :lnk "<9:14>")))
```

I turned off properties, like `:NUM sg`, etc., to keep the graphs tidy. The `:lnk "<4:8>"` relations are character alignments. Let me know if you want any explanation of how our use of variables, predicates (concepts), or relations differs from AMR.

Note: the PyDelphin conversion to EDS does not use all EDS features, but the results should still be valid. Also see <http://moin.delph-in.net/EdsTop#Alternative_Serializations> for Stephan Oepen's version; his (using the LKB instead of ACE, and with support for all EDS features) is available via the web-API, e.g.:

```
http://erg.delph-in.net/rest/0.9/parse?input="Kim gave Sandy the book."&eds=amr
```
(sorry, no frontend for it right now).

Here's a few more examples from PyDelphin:

```lisp
; "The cheerful children wanted to sing and dance."
(e2 / _want_v_1
    :lnk "<22:28>"
    :ARG1 (x3 / _child_n_1
              :lnk "<13:21>"
              :BV-of (_1 / _the_q
                         :lnk "<0:3>")
              :ARG1-of (e8 / _cheerful_a_1
                           :lnk "<4:12>"))
    :ARG2 (e14 / _and_c
               :lnk "<37:40>"
               :L-INDEX (e11 / _sing_v_1
                             :lnk "<32:36>"
                             :ARG1 x3)
               :R-INDEX (e15 / _dance_v_1
                             :lnk "<41:47>"
                             :ARG1 x3)
               :L-HNDL e11
               :R-HNDL e15))

; "This technique is impossible to apply."
(e2 / _impossible_a_for
    :lnk "<18:28>"
    :ARG1 (e11 / _apply_v_2
               :lnk "<32:38>"
               :ARG2 (x3 / _technique_n_1
                         :lnk "<5:14>"
                         :BV-of (_1 / _this_q_dem
                                    :lnk "<0:4>"))))

; "All dogs chased a cat."
(e2 / _chase_v_1
    :lnk "<9:15>"
    :ARG1 (x3 / _dog_n_1
              :lnk "<4:8>"
              :BV-of (_1 / _all_q
                         :lnk "<0:3>"))
    :ARG2 (x8 / _cat_n_1
              :lnk "<18:22>"
              :BV-of (_2 / _a_q
                         :lnk "<16:17>")))
```

#### DMRS 

DMRS is very similar to EDS but retains scope information through secondary edge labels. Here are the same examples (just change the `--to` option of `delphin.sh` to use `dmrs-penman`).

```lisp
; "Kim gave Sandy the book."
(10002 / _give_v_1
       :lnk "<4:8>"
       :ARG1-NEQ (10001 / named
                        :lnk "<0:3>"
                        :carg "Kim"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10000 / proper_q
                                          :lnk "<0:3>"))
       :ARG3-NEQ (10004 / named
                        :lnk "<9:14>"
                        :carg "Sandy"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10003 / proper_q
                                          :lnk "<9:14>"))
       :ARG2-NEQ (10006 / _book_n_of
                        :lnk "<19:24>"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10005 / _the_q
                                          :lnk "<15:18>")))

; "The cheerful children wanted to sing and dance."
(10003 / _want_v_1
       :lnk "<22:28>"
       :ARG1-NEQ (10002 / _child_n_1
                        :lnk "<13:21>"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10000 / _the_q
                                          :lnk "<0:3>")
                        :ARG1-EQ-of (10001 / _cheerful_a_1
                                           :lnk "<4:12>"))
       :ARG2-H (10005 / _and_c
                      :lnk "<37:40>"
                      :L-HNDL-HEQ (10004 / _sing_v_1
                                         :lnk "<32:36>"
                                         :ARG1-NEQ 10002)
                      :L-INDEX-NEQ 10004
                      :R-HNDL-HEQ (10006 / _dance_v_1
                                         :lnk "<41:47>"
                                         :ARG1-NEQ 10002)
                      :R-INDEX-NEQ 10006))

; "This technique is impossible to apply."
(10002 / _impossible_a_for
       :lnk "<18:28>"
       :ARG1-H (10003 / _apply_v_2
                      :lnk "<32:38>"
                      :ARG2-NEQ (10001 / _technique_n_1
                                       :lnk "<5:14>"
                                       :RSTR-H-of (10000 / _this_q_dem
                                                         :lnk "<0:4>"))))

; "All dogs chased a cat."
(10002 / _chase_v_1
       :lnk "<9:15>"
       :ARG1-NEQ (10001 / _dog_n_1
                        :lnk "<4:8>"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10000 / _all_q
                                          :lnk "<0:3>"))
       :ARG2-NEQ (10004 / _cat_n_1
                        :lnk "<18:22>"
                        :RSTR-H-of (10003 / _a_q
                                          :lnk "<16:17>")))
```

Sadly, DMRS is not available via Stephan's server. Also see [this utility](https://github.com/goodmami/mrs-to-penman) I created for some experiments doing neural generation from PENMAN-serialized DMRS. We used the utility to convert the Redwoods corpus.

Actually this is worse than I initially thought it would be.  When somebody on conda they will:
  * `git pull`
  * `conda info`  # oh, this is broken now saying auxlib is missing
  * `conda install auxlib` # not found (even if it was available, they'd had to `git revert`)
  * `pip install auxlib` # hmm, we tell people to pip install stuff?
  * `conda info` # Yay!  It finally works.

I'm not complaining here, as I can work through this.  But many develops may find all very confusing.

I suggest that we put auxlib (or the pieces we need from it) into the conda source root.  This will also mean that the `import auxlib` in `setup.py` will always work.

Looking at the internals here, this is due to the way `conda` attempts to merge two different types of version specs. 

The double-equal `==` specs are interpreted "pip-style", and pip-style specs do not support asterisk wildcards. If you want to select the latest `1.8` version in this manner, you should use something like this: `numpy >=1.8,<1.9`

The single-equal `=` spec is interpreted using a newer conda convention that does allow for wildcards. So `numpy =1.8.*` is indeed equivalent to `numpy >=1.8,<1.9` in this case.

What is interesting is that *internally*, `numpy =1.8.*` is converted to `numpy 1.8.*`. So there's nothing internally that would prevent `conda` from supporting option (3). It blocks during the pattern matching.

So it looks like ``carray.__getitem__`` has some overhead which can almost double the time to run a chunk-wise computation. [This notebook](http://nbviewer.ipython.org/gist/alimanfoo/88a937ef0953f4a3c5d1/bcolz_profile_getitem.ipynb) has some profiling data for computation of a sum over a carray arange. Again I compared carray.sum() which accesses each chunk directly, my own simple chunked_sum() which has to go through ``carray.__getitem__`` to retrieve each chunk, and da.sum() which also has to go via ``carray.__getitem__``.

With current bcolz, both my chunked_sum and dask spend as much time in ``carray.__getitem__`` (``carray_ext.pyx:1855(__getitem__)``) as they do within ``chunk._getitem`` (``carray_ext.pyx:467(_getitem)``) where decompression actually occurs. 

I believe this overhead arises for two reasons. Firstly, ``carray.__getitem__`` sets up a numpy array for the result, then calls ``chunk._getitem`` which sets up it's own numpy array for the decompressed chunk data. So memory for the decompressed data is allocated and filled twice. Secondly, ``carray.__getitem__`` currently iterates over every single chunk in order to figure out which chunks actually have data needed to fulfill the request. This iteration and the calls to ``clip_chunk`` within each loop costs a bit of time. 

In the case where a simple slice is requested with no step, it is possible to store the decompressed data from each chunk directly into the outer numpy array created to hold the overall result. This saves double memory allocation. 

For slices it is also possible to figure out the first and last chunk required, and so avoid iterating over every single chunk.

After making these modifications, my chunked_sum is exactly the same speed as carray.sum(). All tests also pass. da.sum() with the synchronous scheduler is also exactly the same speed. 

(Aside: da.sum() with the threaded scheduler and only 1 worker is very slow in both cases, but that is probably a different issue.)

I have included the changes to ``carray.__getitem__`` (ce38653) in this branch to help understand the maximum potential of dask with bcolz, however if you would like to see these changes broken into a separate PR then just say.

This is used to compare file paths, to see if a file is already opened or not. I use `os.path.normcase` which only have an effect on windows. @untergeek Using Curator against Elastic Cloud will reproduce the behavior. 

```
cat config.yml
client:
  hosts:
    - 9a6ed25bbc72800abad46167e3fb5152.us-east-1.aws.found.io:9243
  use_ssl: True
  ssl_no_validate: False
  http_auth: some_user:anyonesguess
```

```
/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/elasticsearch/connection/http_urllib3.py:70: UserWarning: Connecting to 9a6ed25bbc72800abad46167e3fb5152.us-east-1.aws.found.io using SSL with verify_certs=False is insecure.
  'Connecting to %s using SSL with verify_certs=False is insecure.' % host)
/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/urllib3/connectionpool.py:838: InsecureRequestWarning: Unverified HTTPS request is being made. Adding certificate verification is strongly advised. See: https://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/security.html
  InsecureRequestWarning)
```

From my research, it looks like `urllib3` needs a reference to the OS CA bundle since, by default, it does not do any certificate verification. This configuration on CentOS7 allows Curator to work with EC's public cert without warnings:

```
certificate: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-bundle.crt
```

Alternatively, if I only want EC's trust in my Curator config, then I can place its CA root and intermediate in a bundle and configure the `certificate` setting:

```
  ssl_no_validate: False
  certificate: /opt/es-curator/es-cloud-ca-bundle.pem
```

This will again ignore the OS bundle since, by default, it is not referenced by `urllib3` and uses the `certificate` configuration for verification. I have not tested it yet, but the same should be true for self-signed certificates since `urllib3` makes no distinction.

Hopefully we can at least better document how to make this work.

Try wrapping the * in single quotes?

Because we currently pass arguments to the sdk by wrapping them all up in a dictionary and giving them as kwargs. If an argument is `None`, it will override the default value in the sdk to be `None` as well.

To solve this issue, we would need to add checks in the methods for those args, and only pass them along if they are not `None`. This would need to be done for each method, of which there are several, and for every parameter that has a default value in the sdk, not just `max_connections`. 

All in all, I'll probably do this, I raised this issue for tracking as it is not the point of the [pr](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/pull/5384) where I saw this.

The prior on the transition matrix/starting probability is indeed a Dirichlet (you can think of it as specifying pseudocounts).

To have a prior on the mean and covariance of a multivariate Gaussian, you would use a normal(-inverse) Wishart distribution. The M-step in `GaussianHMM` is based on this, however, you do not specify the parameters of the prior distribution but rather the variables `covars_prior`, `covars_weight`, `means_prior`, and `means_weight`.  If you want to know how these are related to the parameters of the normal(-inverse) Wishart, you can go about it by deriving the MAP solution and  relating it to the parametrization in the package. 

You might find it helpful to look at: _Maximum A Posteriori Estimation for Multivariate Gaussian Mixture Observations of Markov Chains_ by Gauvain and Lee (1993).

Hi guys, I'm working the issue. It seems:

+ the issue is only related to the hotword sample `google-assistant-demo`. `googlesamples-assistant-push-to-talk` works well.
+ It happened not just on the respeaker 2 mic hat, but also on some USB audio card (from pradeepprakash's comment)

There are two solutions:

1. When using [seeed prebuild image](https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/wiki.seeed.cc/001share/seeed-raspbian-jessie-20170523.7z) , place [the ALSA configuration file](https://github.com/respeaker/seeed-voicecard/raw/master/asound.conf) to `/etc/asound.conf`
   ```
   wget https://github.com/respeaker/seeed-voicecard/raw/master/asound.conf
   sudo cp asound.conf /etc/asound.conf
   ```
   The alsa configuration uses dmix plugin and dsnoop plugin to support multiple applications to play and record simultaneously.

2. Use pulseaudio. On Raspbian Jessie, pulseaudio will properly detect the voice card. But on Raspbian Stretch, pulseaudio may only detect the voice card as an input device, try the following pulseaudio configuration:

   ```
   wget https://github.com/respeaker/seeed-voicecard/raw/master/default.pa
   sudo cp default.pa /etc/pulse
   ```

When I use the latest Raspbian (2017-08-16) with the respeaker 2 mic hat,  `google-assistant-demo` didn't have the speed issue by default, but the response voice is very low. Using one of the two solutions would solve it.

If you still have the issue after having tried the two solutions,  you can join [the gitter chat room to discuss](https://gitter.im/seeed-voicecard/Lobby?utm_source=share-link&utm_medium=link&utm_campaign=share-link)

@fperez sorry if my response was curt. This is a classic bug that's really annoying for the people it affects, but I still maintain that the vast majority of the new users you're concerned about will not be affected. Obviously we will take any bug you file fairly seriously however it's worded, but I find your bug reports sometimes have a little bit of the "this must be fixed now" flavour. And I find that all the more surprising because you have been the one reading and responding to plenty of bug reports in the past, so you know what makes a good one from the maintainers' point of view.

Back to the issue:

* To tackle the specific issue that caused your problem, maybe if $BROWSER is set and launching a browser fails, we should assume it's set badly, and try again as if it wasn't set. Scanning through the code of the `webbrowser` module, there doesn't seem to be a good way to do that, but if we think it will affect enough users, I'm sure we can come up with something.
* I totally agree that it's a poor user experience if the server doesn't launch the browser. But I don't see a great way to improve this in general. AIUI, we need to get a secret to the browser. If we can launch the browser, we do that via the command line. If not, we make it available in the terminal and on the filesystem for the user to pick up. We can't transfer it over HTTP (unless I'm missing a trick), because that's the channel we're trying to secure. We can definitely make it clearer what's going on, which we're doing in 4.3.1, but I don't see a way to avoid it.
* If we can't launch the browser and the user isn't looking at a terminal (e.g. because they've launched the notebook from a GUI), we don't have any obvious way to communicate with them even to point out what's going wrong. We could try to find an available GUI toolkit (tkinter is usually there) and open a window with information. This feels very much like a band-aid solution, though.
* We could try to push users into setting a password, so that they know what to enter on the login page. E.g. the Anaconda launcher could get the user to set a password when they first launch the notebook. But this isn't great UX either, and it's extra work for less security (human chosen passwords are easier to guess than a generated token).
* We could provide a GUI [notebook server manager](https://github.com/takluyver/nbmanager) as part of a default install, and add options to launch a browser and to copy the URL from that interface. That gets us back into the local GUI development we've avoided by making a web interface, but maybe that's OK for a tool with much more limited scope.

This code is a WIP but does work:

A ContentsManager that passes to other ContentsManagers who also have a manages_path function:
```
from notebook.services.contents.filemanager import ContentsManager

def make_dispatched(self, function):
    """
    This function inspects a function and dispatches it out by finding which
    arg is the path, then finding a manager to manage that path and forwarding
    this on
    """
    # functions passed here have self in it, when it's called its bound so -1 for no self
    path_idx = inspect.getargspec(function)[0].index('path') - 1

    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        path = kwargs['path'] if 'path' in kwargs else args[path_idx]
        for manager in self.managers:
            if manager.manages_path(path):
                fn = getattr(manager, function.__name__)
                return fn(*args, **kwargs)
        # This self is jupyter's
        self.log.error("Could not find manager for path %s", path)
        raise Exception('Could not find manager for path "%s"' % path)

    return wrapper


_managers = None
def set_managers(managers):
    """
    We need a global list because I dont see where to pass this data to juptyer->manager
    """
    global _managers
    _managers = managers


class DispatchedContentsManager(ContentsManager):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        global _managers
        self.managers = []
        for manager in _managers:
            # Init each manger with the same params
            inst = manager(**kwargs)
            if not hasattr(inst, 'manages_path'):
                raise RuntimeError('Count not find method manages_path in manager!')
            self.managers.append(inst)

        # FIXME: add support for rename_file! This does not follow the pattern below
        for name, method in inspect.getmembers(self,
                                               predicate=inspect.ismethod):
            if (not name.startswith('_') and
                        'path' in inspect.getargspec(method)[0]):
                setattr(self, name, make_dispatched(self, method))
```

Example manager (typically manages_path has a regexp in it):
```
from notebook.services.contents.filemanager import FileContentsManager

class DefaultFileManager(FileContentsManager):
    """
    A fallback manager to be used for all files
    """

    def manages_path(self, path):
        return True
```

Now in a jupyter config:

```
set_managers([DefaultFileManager]) # Pass in the class
```

I would be happy to create a pull request, but I'm unsure what direction you would like to head in here.

In bd0f14a1f30873de65db22019a8644142437ed3a is_reachable was changed from socket.inet_aton(ip) to requests.get(ip).  This completely changes the behaviour of the function.  

Before the change, inet_aton(ip) was used, which simply converts a ipv4 style IP address to 32-bit packed binary format.  It's possible the intent here was simply to validate that the IP address is valid, in which case I would argue that the function should be renamed to something more appropriate, and should not be used [here](https://github.com/mnubo/kubernetes-py/blob/b5b7da2c4d324fb3be267506742b842b4c7c8756/kubernetes/K8sCronJob.py#L23) since config.api_host is not necessarily an IP address.

After the change, requests.get(ip) is used, which actually performs a query against the provided URL.  This has two problems. First, it does not make use of the certs provided by the user, [unlike other calls to the kubernetes server](https://github.com/mnubo/kubernetes-py/blob/b5b7da2c4d324fb3be267506742b842b4c7c8756/kubernetes/K8sObject.py#L174).  Second, it is used in cases that before were simply passing in IP addresses.  For example, [is_reachable is run on the the external IPs in a Service](https://github.com/mnubo/kubernetes-py/blob/b5b7da2c4d324fb3be267506742b842b4c7c8756/kubernetes/models/v1/ServiceSpec.py#L85).  As far as I know, external IPs are usually a simply IP address, not a URL, and requests.get does not work on an IP (at least in python 2.7):
```python
>>> requests.get("127.0.0.1")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\api.py", line 70, in get
    return request('get', url, params=params, **kwargs)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\api.py", line 56, in request
    return session.request(method=method, url=url, **kwargs)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\sessions.py", line 474, in request
    prep = self.prepare_request(req)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\sessions.py", line 407, in prepare_request
    hooks=merge_hooks(request.hooks, self.hooks),
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\models.py", line 302, in prepare
    self.prepare_url(url, params)
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\requests\models.py", line 382, in prepare_url
    raise MissingSchema(error)
requests.exceptions.MissingSchema: Invalid URL '127.0.0.1': No schema supplied. Perhaps you meant http://127.0.0.1?
```

My suggestion would be rolling back the behaviour of is_reachable to use inet_aton, and renaming it to is_valid_ip.  Then, create a new function (possibly in K8sObject.py so it can take advantage of the [existing request function](https://github.com/mnubo/kubernetes-py/blob/b5b7da2c4d324fb3be267506742b842b4c7c8756/kubernetes/K8sObject.py#L174) there) called is_reachable that checks if the Kubernetes host is reachable, and then use that function instead of the [newly added calls to is_reachable](https://github.com/mnubo/kubernetes-py/commit/d4c7650169325d8fbb8faa2de0e7b668cab7d3c6) in K8sCronJob.py and K8sPetSet.py.

Alternatively, instead of checking that the server is reachable ahead of time, the call to temp.server_version could simply be wrapped in a try... Except: pass

Let me know if you like the idea and I'll get working on a merge request.

You can use the `getval` function to do this. Basically, `getval` tells autograd to "consider this thing a constant" by unwrapping it from any tracing that autograd may be doing:

```python
from autograd import grad, getval

def g(x): return 2 * x
def f(x): return x * g(x)  # note only one argument, this evaluates f(g(x))

print grad(f)(1.)  # 4.0

def f2(x): return x * getval(g(x))
print grad(f2)(1.)  # 2.0
```

However, you can instead just use how partial derivatives work:

```python
def g(x): return 2 * x
def f(x, y): return x * y  # note two arguments, as in your code

x = 1.
print grad(f)(x, g(x))  # 2.0
```

My hunch is that wanting to use `getval`/`stop_gradient` is an artifact of thinking in terms of explicitly graph-based libraries (like Theano and TensorFlow), and correspondingly an artifact of thinking about differentiating variables with respect to other variables, leaving implicit the functions you're actually differentiating. By contrast, in autograd you take partial derivatives of functions, and it's easy to reason about differentiating with respect to one argument while holding the others fixed: that's what a partial derivative is!

Still, using `getval` might be more convenient, depending on how one's code is structured.

For another argument about where autograd-like conventions can be better, see the Prologue section of Sussman and Wisdom's [Functional Differential Geometry](https://www.dropbox.com/s/t3si4b99ijqyhyk/9580.pdf?dl=0) (that's the official PDF from MIT Press; it's an open-access book). (The same argument is also in the Preface section of their [Structure and Interpretation of Classical Mechanics](https://mitpress.mit.edu/sites/default/files/titles/content/sicm/book.html).) Notice that differentiation is of functions and is taken with respect to their positional arguments, rather than the more common convention of writing differentiation in terms of named input and output symbols. I think this positional argument notation comes from Spivak's Calculus on Manifolds from 1965. It really cleans up one's thinking about differentiation; the only problem with it is that it's not standard.

Hello Jonas
that is indeed the problem. The allele classification needs to be queue and our efforts has been in parallelising the allele sequence extraction first and then create a queue for the allele naming. That way multiple processes can run the allele sequence extraction in parallel but the allele ID attribution still needs to be done by batch following a queue. We don't plan yet to abandon the file based approach and we think that this 2-step approach will avoid data corruption. We are also working on a centralised nomenclature server where authorized user can synch new alleles . We have the first part done (the allele sequence extraction) but we still need to implement the second part. It may take a few weeks still since we have other things on the queue first.

The goal here is to ensure that the Nginx plugin can always restore your config. If the plugin errors out while performing an operation, all in progress changes should be reverted. Additionally, the user should be able to run `letsencrypt rollback` to undo changes caused by the Nginx plugin on previous runs of the client.

To do this, we need to ensure the Nginx plugin uses the `reverter` properly which I have verified it isn't. I don't think there are too many problems, but I found at least one after some quick testing.

There are three main cases we care about: `perform`, `deploy_cert` and `enhance`. Common to all of these cases, `letsencrypt` first instantiates the Nginx plugin and then calls `prepare`. After this, the functions that may have been called on the Nginx plugin start to diverge.

Modifying function(s) | Recovery function | Desired outcome
----------------------------- |--------------------------|-------------------------
`perform` | `cleanup` | After `cleanup` is called, either when `perform` returns or raises an exception, all changes to the Nginx configuration should be reverted. Running `letsencrypt certonly` with Nginx should NEVER leave permanent changes to the user's config.
`deploy_cert` followed by `save` | `recovery_routine` | Similar to the example above, `recovery_routine` is called if `deploy_cert`/`save` raises an exception. When this occurs, all changes to the Nginx configuration should be reverted and the user's Nginx config should be identical to before their last run of the client.
`enhance` followed by `save` | `recovery_routine` | Same as above, except `enhance` can only by a user after calling `deploy_cert` + `save`. When `recovery_routine` is called here, their config should be reverted to the state after the calls to `deploy_cert` and `save`.

You don't have to worry about calling the recovery function in the table above. The core client handles that for you, however, you need to make sure that all of the changes you made are reverted properly.

The error I found occurred in the `perform` case. On a Debian Jessie docker image, I installed `nginx-light` and ran `letsencrypt certonly --nginx`, providing valid domain names when prompted. When `letsencrypt` exited, my config had been modified.

I hope this all makes sense. Please let me know if you have any questions.

> Telegram seems to send 8 bytes of data periodically

Those 8 bytes of data are length of the packet and sequence number:
https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/blob/8a9650f131e1cfed8d1b0e6c818eab9230741ab9/telethon/network/connection/tcpfull.py#L44-L55

*Every* packet has that by default unless you use another connection mode, so it clearly should always alternate.

> There are some orphaned responses

That's something we can check with:

```python
from telethon.extensions import BinaryReader

with BinaryReader(b"\xd5...") as reader:
    print(reader.tgread_object())
    for _ in range(4):
        print(reader.read_int())
    print(reader.tgread_object())
    for _ in range(6):
        print(reader.read_int())
```

And some trial and error since we know everything is aligned to 4 bytes, which is first https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/issues/658 and then a `MsgsAck`, thing I didn't know could happen. OK, I found the cause of the false positive. The compiler packs both the `address public owner` and `bool public FLAG` into storage slot 0. It looks similar to this:

```
0x[000..][FLAG (1 byte)][owner (20 bytes)]
```

Mythril sees that `setFlag` writes to the same slot and erroneously assumes that this affects the `owner` variable.

Unfortunately the analysis of storage operations is still pretty dumb. I have some ideas for improving it though, and hopefully will come up with something in the next couple of weeks!

I also get this error message if I use curl and add text/html as an accepted mime type. 
I would suggest to remove the [http](https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/blob/master/tastypie/serializers.py#L86) from the list of supported formats. The list of supported formats is passed to the [best_match](https://github.com/dbtsai/python-mimeparse/blob/master/mimeparse.py#L137) function. Because tastypie passes `text/html` to the list of supported formats it will return `text/html` as the preferred format (because it has the default [q=1](http://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html)), but it is in fact not supported (yet). 

This is an example of a curl request that fails, even though it supports json:
```
curl -H "Accept: application/json;q=0.9,text/html" http://localhost:8000/api/v1/
```

You also might be interested in using `DependenciesContainer` provider that gives you a chance to make late static binding. Please, take a look on next example:

```python
class Drivers(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    services = providers.DependenciesContainer()

    data_driver = providers.Singleton(
        DataDriver,
        api_services=services.api_service
    ) # type: Callable[[], APIDataDriver]

class Services:
    api_service = providers.Singleton(
        APIService,
        driver=Drivers.api_driver
    ) # type: Callable[[], APIService]

    data_service = providers.Singleton(
        DataService,
        driver=Drivers.data_driver
    ) # type: Callable[[], DataService]


# In a runtime...

services = Services()
drivers = Drivers(services=services)

data_driver = drivers.data_driver()
```

Ok so, the situation from the ocaml perspective (and in particular, the desire to package z3 and its API in opam, the usual package manager for ocaml which is a *source* package manager) is, as far as I can see the following:
- if distribs don't package z3 (and they don't for now, but I expect it to change) a z3 opam package have to recompile z3 in a whole and so the package will contains its own version of libz3.so, hence linking statically in libz3ml seems acceptable to me. This means long-ish compilation time, but is very flexible.
- if distribs do start packaging z3, they will probably don't distribute the ml package (and it's preferable to have it in opam anyway, to handle multiple ocaml versions). In this case, dynamic linking against the system z3 is the preferable way, but in this case, it would be much more convenient to be able to recompile the ml binding against a system z3 (instead of recompiling libz3.so before, as it's done currently). This is how llvm in-tree bindings are packaged in opam currently. This means installing dev versions is a bit of a sport but makes the package very fast to compile.

I'm personally fine with either solution (both have great merits) but you need to choose one. The current state is slightly sitting in the middle and is very inappropriate for separate packaging of z3 and the ml API.

Note that I *don't* ask you to officially support opam or any package manager in any way, just give a way to package it cleanly. ;)

- if validating a bundle, use the version specified in the bundle.

- Allow users to specify an explicit version via a command line argument or function parameter.

> the factor and the fixed output shape would result in different behaviour for variable input sizes (the factor would adapt to the size, the fixed shape would be, well, fixed).

But a fixed shape is what you want, no? Except in the special case where the network you stack on top of it is fully convolutional and you have some kind of global pooling layer at the end.

So I don't really understand how being able to specify a scale factor exempts you from knowing the input shape -- you still need to know what shape the output of the `TransformerLayer` is going to be anyway in most cases. You just have to do the calculation in your head, which seems wasteful :)

> But did you fully appreciate the impact of what happens if you do [l.get_output() for l in my_output_layers] instead of get_output(my_output_layers)? (#97, #104) Having get_output() as a Layer method would invite this use, reopening a pitfall that we intended to close.

I am not proud of it, but apparently I was using `get_output` exactly in the way you wanted to prevent. Moving the methods outside of `Layer` will not prevent a motivated user from using them in the wrong way! ;)

>It would require memoization then, with all its disadvantages (memoizing multiple variants for the different keyword arguments and input layers, taking care about properly using weak references to avoid leaks, increasing code complexity). Anyway, I love design discussions! :)

The disadvantages of memoization don't seem dramatic to me, you would have to memoize multiple symbolic variables for each symbolic input *to the current Layer* (e.g. comparing hashes) and for the tags *that affect the current layer*. One Layer does not need to know what was the input to the InputLayer and which Layers came before it, it just needs to know if it has a symbolic output in its cache for its current symbolic inputs and tags (only those that affect it, though).

> OK -- I struggled to implement a Layer class that wraps some other layers in a nice way, it felt like having these get_* functions as methods would have made it more natural (I could override them to delegate to the equivalent methods on the layers I wrap), but sounds like I'm missing a better way, I'm not particularly hung up on it.

I struggled a lot to achieve the same, which is why I opened this issue! To have some methods to override to delegate them to the sublayers I was wrapping was my wish exactly. 

I ended up doing this, which seems to work:
```python
class SomeClass(Layer):
    def __init__(self,
        # some computation
        # ....
        self.l_out = the_last_sublayer

        # HACK LASAGNE
        # This will set `self.input_layer`, which is needed by Lasagne to find
        # the layers with the get_all_layers() helper function in the
        # case of a layer with sublayers
        if isinstance(self.l_out, tuple):
            self.input_layer = None
        else:
            self.input_layer = self.l_out

    def get_output_for(self, input_var, **kwargs):
        # HACK LASAGNE
        # This is needed, jointly with the previous hack, to ensure that
        # this layer behaves as its last sublayer (namely,
        # self.input_layer)
        return input_var
```

> I don't know if there is a canonical for these kind of tasks in redux.

## Non-redux, had we stuck with full immutable

If the full structure was Immutable.js, then you'd only store the `notebook.hashCode()` for the saved notebook and compare it to the `notebook.hashCode()` for the current notebook. I'm only noting this to show what the benefit of truly immutable data structures are (when deeply immutable).

## Redux way

I _think_ the way to do this is to register certain kinds of changes as `dirty` for the notebook. Then, everytime someone has a successful save we set `dirty = false` (in the state tree).

@ltfish

Minimal example will be like this:

    start:
      call input
    a:
      test eax, eax
      jz target
      jmp b
    b:
      jmp c
    c:
      jmp target
    target:
      [merge here]

(Let's assume `input` returns unconstrained value in `eax`)
Here, two paths will have this trace (current IP is of course excluded):

    Path "input() == 0" (ip=target):
        addr_trace.hardcopy: start, a
    Path "input() != 0" (ip=target):
        addr_trace.hardcopy: start, a, b, c

When we attempt to merge these two paths, it first applies `zip` to those traces, which results in:

    [(start, start), (a, a)]

There are no difference except the latter one being longer, hence `ValueError` thrown by `.index(False)`.

CLE does have a mechanism for loading core dumps.

the `4` is just an autoincrementing identifier. It exists only to make sure that names are all unique. If you really, truly need to disable this, you can pass `explicit_names=True` into the BVS constructor, I think? But generally, if you're ever working directly with variable *names*,  you're doing something wrong. Claripy ASTs are supposed to have what's called "object identity", which means that once you create an object, it's immutable, and that object should be the only way to refer to the entity you just created. So if you wanted to represent the above example, you wouldn't want to have two separate `r0 = state.se.BVS('r0') lines, you would have only one of those, and then you would reuse that one `r0` value for your whole program.

So basically the dependency tree needs to try to get composite dependencies at the same resolution as the other inputs. If it can't find the resolution it can fall back to the highest resolution. A couple design questions:

1. Does this happen for modifiers *and* composites?
2. If for composites, the only way this makes sense is if we find the first dependency and then say we want the other dependencies at that resolution. So we want B01, B02, B03 for our composite. We get B01 at 1000m, then we look for B02 at 1000m, it is returned at 1000m even though there is a 250m available. Is that expected behavior? Is that right? I suppose if we just check for the highest resolution of each channel then the composite just has to handle that.

I can foresee issues where one composite's dependencies result in the 1000m SZA being loaded and another composite wants an unavailable 250m resolution so it will then see 1000m is already loaded and use that instead of loading the 500m resolution that would be a better choice. Maybe...not sure.

The goal is to rewrite `hyper` so that it uses `hyper-h2` (which is a vastly better HTTP/2 implementation than the one `hyper` has), and `hyper-h2` has `hpack` as a dependency. At that point, installing `hyper` will give you the following dependency graph:

```
- hyper
|
|-- hyper-h2
  |
  |-- hpack
  |-- hyperframe
```

Each of those will be installed as their own library directly, rather than being bundled into any of the parent libraries.

WHATWG doesn't care much about the slashes since they're just "the things that come after http://" :) but 3986 specifically documents them; they are the slashes that come before the authority.

Which… actually raises the question.  They always and only come before the authority.  The scheme attribute is just whether or not it has an authority.  Which is just a validation thing: the selected scheme should be able to prevent you from using an authority, but the information about the scheme isn't necessary past that point.  So the attribute seems like it ought to be useless.

In fact, reading the implementation alerted me that there's a bug in this area (another failed `asText` / `fromText` round trip):

```python
>>> URL().replace(scheme=u'http', path=[u'',u'',u'google.com'])
URL.from_text(u'http://google.com')
```

Ok, so here are some current problems:

1. The LHCb dirac setup can take some time so we want to cache it in some way.
2. The GangaLHCb and GangaDirac environments are currently dealt with in very different ways.
3. When installing Dirac as a user, you have to do the `env > envfile` dance manually.

Here's a proposal:

Both GangaLHCb and GangaDirac have "something we source" (`SetupProject.sh LHCBDIRAC v8r2p27 ROOT` and `dirac_ui/bashrc`). If we source that and cache the result in a Python-readable format (probably a pickle). We can then read that cache in `getDiracEnv()` in a consistent way. The pickle cache is persisted across sessions and within one session, the env is stored in memory.

What do you think? Would this fit into the cmake world?

Well, it is like if I would use sort of a queueing system. Someone will add a "job" to be consumed, and someone else would consume the job. 
But if I do :

    SELECT id WHERE status=pending            # returns ID1
    UPDATE status=running WHERE id=ID1

and that these calls are not atomic, 2 or more consumers might retrieve the ID1 and therefore duplicate the job and also override what the other just updated.

I need atomic select and update, but not based on ID. This is not something that Restful APIs usually give the ability to do so.

In SQL, it would relate to a SELECT FOR UPDATE statement. In MongoDB it is a findAndModify statement.

I guess with transaction we would be near from what I want, but I don't know if I can use the retrieved ID variable in a second update statement - not sure I am clear in that sentence.

Thanks for your answer.

Since this issue was filed, I've only seen tests fail due to time-outs once: when Pulp services legitimately broke and were failing to function.

We can further increase the timeout window if need be. However, I'm wary of doing so: there's little difference between an unresponsive service and a dead service, and if it takes more than two minutes for Pulp to perform an action, I'm inclined to call that a bug. If we really do need to increase the timeout window, I'd like to also make sure to improve the time-out mechanism in Pulp Smash itself. Right now, Pulp Smash waits 120s *in addition to* whatever time is consumed communicating with the Pulp server. It'd be better if the time-out mechanism is asynchronous and interrupts whatever Pulp Smash is doing. In other words, this code is naive and should be improved if we want to also increase the timout:

## If you mean the React-Relay dependency
The Relay dependency in the client it's not necessary even if you're using the Relay spec in your backend.
So you can use redux (apollo-client) in your client application with Graphene (and graphene relay).

Why is that? Relay is actually two things:
 * [A GraphQL server specification](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/connections.htm)
 * [A GraphQL client for react](https://facebook.github.io/relay/)

In this sense, Graphene only implements the server specification, so you can use the client you want in the frontend (Relay, apollo-client, ...)

## If you mean using a List instead of Relay Connections in the model relations
Yes, there is a way for skipping the Relay Connections conversion!

The `SQLAlchemy` to `graphene` types converter [checks if the `SQLAlchemyObjectType` implements the `relay.Node` type](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/graphene-sqlalchemy/graphene_sqlalchemy/converter.py#L33-L35), and in this case it uses a connection whenever a relation to the model defined there is found.

So for skipping this automatic Relay Connection creation, just don't implement the `relay.Node` in the `SQLAlchemyObjectType` :).

Hi @matanster,

You are correct, we did not use sentence segmentation in our preprocessing. In my experience, I did not observe significant differences with or without sentence segmentation. The version of the tokenizer that we used do tokenize end-of-sentence period symbols.

For other languages, please see our latest release for details about which tokenizers were used: https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/crawl-vectors.html (for the Wikipedia-based multilingual vectors, we used a very crude preprocessing, which can be replicated using the following script: https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/get-wikimedia.sh).

Best,
Edouard

Ah yes, it was to wrap the `copy.copy()` inside `array_type`.

    def custom_copy(array_to_copy):
        array_copy = array_to_copy.copy()
        array_copy.flags.writeable = False
        return array_copy

(defined inside the test method).

Note that `copy.copy()` is used in `_validate` to cope with lists being passed in, which have not `.copy()` method.

As far as I can see, you are making both an executable and an extension module. The first error is because the module is not linked with the dependencies. Add this line to resolve it:
```cmake
target_link_libraries(kakoune PRIVATE ${Boost_LIBRARIES} ${CURSES_LIBRARY})
```

The second error is because the executable should not contain `PYBIND11_PLUGIN`, only the module should. Make a new .cc file (e.g. `src_module/main.cc`) and place `PYBIND11_PLUGIN` in there. The CMake config should look something like this:
```cmake
add_executable(kak ${kakoune_SOURCES})
file(module_SOURCES src_module/main.cc)
pybind11_add_module(kakoune ${kakoune_SOURCES} ${module_SOURCES})
```

> > @bmerry said:

> That can be the case if a pybind11-exported class is used as a base in Python, i.e. `class PyDerived(CppBase): pass`. The new type would get a `__dict__` and become GC-enabled. It would definitely be best to set up a few test cases which trigger this issue before proceeding.

I don't think that was the case I had. I'll see if I still have a commit of the version of my code that ran into the bug. But it's an interesting case nevertheless: does your suggestion of putting the list in the dict work, given that the dict is part of the subclass rather than the pybind11-managed class?

I'm also a little uneasy about the idea of storing this list in `__dict__`. IMHO with a well-written extension module, it should be impossible for pure-Python code to cause memory corruption; with this approach, Python code could do so by deleting the special key from the dict, or modifying the list it points to.

Some possible alternatives (other than the two approaches already discussed):
1. Only provide the pointer on classes for which GC is enabled, but store it in the class rather than inside `__dict__`. That removes my concern about Python code being able to interfere with it while also removing the overhead from classes that aren't GC-enabled. However, I don't know if this will work when the class gets subclassed in Python.
2. Require a class to opt in to the new behaviour, via a tag class passed to the `class_` constructor. Classes that don't opt in get the old behaviour. I'm not a big fan of that since it's not very easy to tell from a quick look at the code which classes need to be tagged, and the bugs that arise if you miss one are subtle.
3. Turn on the new behaviour by default, but allow classes to opt out to save 4-8 bytes. In really I don't expect the opt-out to get much use since it will be difficult to use correctly, so this ends up being similar to my original proposal.
4. Get rid of weakref support on pybind11 classes / make it opt-in. That will make the change size-neutral, and code that relied on the weakref support only for keep_alive will be fine. Unfortunately it will break code that uses weakref support for other purposes, so it probably can't be done in a minor version update.
Just one last addition: the `PYBIND11_CPP_STANDARD` variable allows the standard to be set explicitly. Someone might want only `-std=c++11` or maybe something newer like `-std=c++1z`.

The issue here is that when multiple inheritance is involved we rely on a constructor compatible with `std::shared_ptr`'s aliasing constructor ([constructor (8) here](http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/memory/shared_ptr/shared_ptr)), which lets us create a holder containing a recast pointer (i.e. an `A *`) that keeps the original `C *` alive (and, if it ends up being the last object, destroys the `C *` rather than the `A *`).

If you add:
```
    MyHolder( const MyHolder &other, T* ptr ) : ptr_(ptr) {}
```
the example will work.  (Your example holder doesn't do any actual memory management, of course; in reality it would need to support the sort of aliasing described above).
A few things to fix, but nothing major: this looks pretty good to me.

I was thinking more of static than dynamic type safety. If I saw code that used a factory function returning `shared_ptr<Base>` being used as a constructor for the Python wrapper of Derived, my first guess would be that either
(a) The factory only ever returns `shared_ptr<Derived>`, and ought to have been declared as such; or
(b) The factory doesn't always return `shared_ptr<Derived>`, and the user either ought to be wrapping Base rather than Derived, or they meant to use a different factory function.

Maybe there are legitimate use cases where one wishes to do this, and get an exception thrown if the Python caller passes a combination of arguments that causes the factory not to spit out a `shared_ptr<Derived>`, but my (prejudiced, non-scientific) feeling is that this would be much rarer than the cases above. Removing the support for it from pybind11 would help catch errors at compile time. It wouldn't prevent users implementing this use case, because the user can write a wrapper around his/her factory function that does the dynamic cast, and perhaps raises a more application-aware error whose message explains why the constructor is failing.

There's nothing wrong with using an Eigen::Ref there--I was mainly pointing out that the comment in the Eigen part about needing a temporary isn't actually the case (no matter whether you use the Eigen::Ref or a pointer).  (It's a different story if you were to do `py::object o = py::cast(x)` and then pass `o` into the function: you'd need to use `py::return_value_policy::reference` to avoid the copy there).

But this is all a bit of a diversion: I think the example itself is great.

Aside from the testing issues, update 3 versus update 2 gives us two concrete things:

1. The `all_of_t` and related templates become much simpler than the required workaround in update 2.
2. We have at least one feature, `overload_cast`, that appears to be impossible with update 2.

If 1 was the only issue we could address it by reintroducing the (ugly) workaround instead of using `std::conjunction`/`std::disjunction`, but 2 is a bigger deal: documented features of pybind simply wouldn't be available under certain compilers.  I think this is a very bad thing, and I'd honestly prefer that we remove features that aren't supported in every environment we support.  But that means we can't have nice things like `overload_cast` because of *fixed* bugs in an older compiler that can't be worked around.

HPKP is severely broken and should not be used IMHO.

HPKP requires at least two fingerprints. One for the live certificate and the other is suppose to be for a backup key. The private key associated with the backup fingerprint MUST NOT be on the same server as active private key but HPKP has no way to enforce it.

If the backup private key is on the same server as the active private key than HPKP is pointless because if the live key is compromised, the backup key likely is compromised too.

In addition for lacking any meaningful way to ensure proper key management, HPKP is also a blind trust on first use system.

This means it only protects the client from an active MITM attack if the client happens to already have a valid fingerprint from the site cached. If the user doesn't already have a valid fingerprint cached, not only will they still be subject to MITM but the attacker can even send their own fingerprint that the browser will blindly trust - preventing the client from being able to connect to the genuine site.

HPKP should not be integrated into LE, it is conceptually flawed. Proper public key pinning should have proper validation of the fingerprint on every use, not blind trust on first use. A mechanism for that exists called DANE and it not only does the key pinning in a better way, but you don't have to pre-generate backup keys so there isn't an issue of how to keep your backup keys safe on a different system until needed.

Beginning with point 2.

Batch customers are not formally implemented in Ciw yet. This is a great idea and I'd like to get working on this soon! There is however a workaround for deterministic arrivals:

Consider the case where 5 customers arrive in a batch ever 100 minutes: Using a `Sequential` arrival distribution, this can be simulated as follows:

```
>>> N = ciw.create_network(
...     Arrival_distributions=[['Sequential', [10.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]]],
...     Service_distributions=[['Deterministic', 0.5]],
...     Number_of_servers=[1]
... )

>>> Q = ciw.Simulation(N)
>>> Q.simulate_until_max_time(25)
>>> recs = Q.get_all_records()
```

We would now expect 5 customers to arrive at date `10`, 5 customers to arrive at date `20`, 5 customers to arrive at `30` and so on. If we run this for `25` time units we would expect 10 customers to go through, but we can also see that they queue up as normal:

```
>>> [r.arrival_date for r in recs]
[10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 10.0, 20.0, 20.0, 20.0, 20.0, 20.0]

>>> [r.service_time for r in recs]
[0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5]

>>> [r.waiting_time for r in recs]
[0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]

>>> [r.exit_date for r in recs]
[10.5, 11.0, 11.5, 12.0, 12.5, 20.5, 21.0, 21.5, 22.0, 22.5]
```

So deterministic batch arrivals *can* be implemented. It isn't very readable and I'd like to develop a wrapper for this to make a `BatchArrival` distribution or something similar to make this feature easier and more intuitive to use.

I think the idea of having better UA strings is not bad, but I'm not a fan of this particular solution, as it only provides a set of statically generated UAs, which doesn't solve the issue of using Streamlink "anonymously" / "transparently". The current `CHROME` UA is pretty much outdated and all of Streamlink's HTTP requests using this UA can be identified pretty easily. Same would be true for this suggested approach, but for a set of static UA strings instead of one.

Having something like `CHROME_LATEST` that gets included during build time by some external library like [fake-useragent](https://github.com/hellysmile/fake-useragent) and changes in each Streamlink release would be much better, since those UAs are not unique to Streamlink and are based on up2date real world usage statistics. A random UA string could also be generated by using this lib, if needed.

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest.

What was expected behaviour in this case?

As far as can be seen from the above, behaviour is as expected. The timeout setting on `run_command` is for per host reading of stdout/stderr.

For the first host, it times out after pre-timeout and before post-timeout (55sec of one sleep + 5 of other out of 60sec timeout). For the second and third hosts by the time their stdout is read, 60 (first host timeout) + at most 60 seconds (second and third host timeouts each) have passed which is over the 55second wait before post-timeout as well as over the overall 55+55 second duration.

Therefore no timeout for second or third host. If this is not what was expected, can you make a smaller test case to demonstrate? As a side note, since commands are the same, `host_args` is not needed.

Hi Rob,

thank you for feedback. In a very short term we are hoping to have this branch merged in it's current state. Changes are backwards compatible with processing apps we currently have and we will likely leave them be as they are for now. Longer term I think we can all agree that writing "task apps" can be a lot simpler, and you can consider work in this branch as an exploration of pain points. I do not claim to address any of them just yet.

Ideally I'd like us to move away from writing custom launch scripts with hard-coded environment setup in bash and using `qsub` on a command-line, I think all this logic can be done in python and re-used by all processing apps with an `import` and a decorator.

I want to be able to write processing app that can be easily tested locally, and then without writing any scripts deployed on NCI, or any other compute provider we might support in the future.

```
  source activate my-env
  # run locally to test
  ./my-processing-app.py --my-app-option1 --my-option2=small-dataset

  # run on a raijin using qsub under the hood
  ./my-processing-app.py --pbs="num-nodes=10,time=1h" --my-app-option1 --my-option2=large-dataset

  # run on a AWS using dockers or whatever 
  ./my-processing-app.py --aws="num-nodes=10,region=oregon" --my-app-option1 --my-option2=large-dataset
```

I will be away in June and everyone else have their plate full with higher priority tasks as it is, so I do not expect any more work to be done in that direction for a while.

> The check_one_way_stream runs a test that ensures that closing the S side of the stream stops the R side from working - with pipes, this can't happen due to child processes and what-not.

This isn't a problem. It would be a problem if we stopped in the middle of a test to spawn a child process and shared our pipe object with us, but we won't do that :-). If you're just using a pipe object within a process, then it follows the usual rule where closing the send side causes the receive side to get an EOF:

```python
In [1]: import os

In [2]: R, S = os.pipe()

In [3]: os.write(S, b"x")
Out[3]: 1

In [4]: os.close(S)

In [5]: os.read(R, 10)
Out[5]: b'x'

In [6]: os.read(R, 10)
Out[6]: b''
```

You are right, it is important to get a clear vocabulary on this:

According to the [product naming convention](https://sentinel.esa.int/web/sentinel/user-guides/sentinel-2-msi/naming-convention) the filename contains two dates:

- **creation date** what I referred to as **production date**: When the L1C product is *created* from raw data at one of the ground segments
- **sensing time**: When the satellite acquired the image.

The metadata of DHuS then contains a third date:
- **ingestion date**: when the L1C product was uploaded, *ingested* into the DHuS

from bs4 import BeautifulSoup as bs

from bgmi.script import ScriptBase

        bangumi_name = 'Rick and Morty Season 3'
        cover = 'http://img.itvfans.com/wp-content/uploads/31346.jpg'
        update_time = 'Wed'

    def get_download_url(self):
        # fetch and return dict
        resp = requests.get('http://www.itvfans.com/fenji/313463.html').text
        html = bs(resp, 'lxml')

        data = html.find(attrs={'id': '31346-3-720p'})

        if not data:
            print_error('No data found, maybe the script is out-of-date.', exit_=False)
            return {}

        ret = {}
        match_episode = re.compile('Rick\.and\.Morty\.S03E(\d+)\.720p')
        for row in data.find_all('a', attrs={'type': 'magnet'}):
            link = row.attrs['href']
            episode = match_episode.findall(link)
            if episode:
                ret[int(episode[0])] = link

        return ret


if __name__ == '__main__':
    s = Script()
    print(s.get_download_url())```

Hey Diego, 

Good catch on the readme.  However, I'm not convinced that we should be shipping a model with django-ajax-uploader, and less so that south should be required. (Note that I love south, and use it in all of my projects, but think that it's out of the scope of what we're aiming for here.)

To merge the local_database backend, I'd like to see:
- the models and migrations should have to be explicitly enabled by a setting. Where the uploaded file goes is business logic, and even though you've used GFK's, they're slow, and might not be wanted by users of the lib.  We shouldn't be adding tables to user's databases unless they've requested them.
- No reason to have two migrations, as far as I can tell.
- Documentation of this backend, particularly of possible schema changes.

Also, if you want to submit those readme changes as a separate pull, I'll merge them right away.

-Steven
